data.title: VGT: Fantastic Holdings, But Just Too Expensive For Our Liking At The Moment
data.description:
Introduction

The Vanguard Information Technology ETF (NYSEARCA:VGT) is a low-cost index fund that aims to track the performance of the Information Technology Spliced Index. Its holdings are primarily large cap tech names, which has done well for investors over the past few years as tech stocks have enjoyed a strong bull run. Below are some details of the fund, taken from the prospectus.

Expense ratio: 0.09%

AUM: $109.7B

Volume: 213K

Their top holdings are shown below.

Their top sector exposures are here.

We like the companies in this fund as they are leaders in their field and top tech names overall. In the following section we will review some news for the top holdings for VGT.

Top Holdings Analysis

Nvidia (NVDA): This is the leading AI chip manufacturer in the world. They have become so dominant that even the President has weighed the idea of breaking up NVDA - but he has decided against it. Jensen Huang, the CEO, is a master at building relationships and he has been able to really get into Trump’s ear to help craft the AI vision for the future, while benefiting shareholders greatly. Earlier this week, Trump hosted an AI action plan event, where leaders such as Huang and AMD’s Lisa Su presented their thoughts on how the US can continue being the world leaders of AI development. It is good to see that the administration is letting some of these top notch companies, both of which are in the top 10 of VGT, help guide the principles on which US lawmakers will govern over AI in the future. There is going to be significant governmental involvement in AI in the coming years as well, which we believe will be very beneficial to these leading firms. NVDA stays poised to benefit from this trend, as do many of the other firms on the top holdings list.

Microsoft (MSFT): MSFT remains a leader both in enterprise computing, but also personal computing. There is a massive catalyst for their personal computing business coming up, as Windows 10 marches towards its end of life in the fall. The following graph shows the growth of the AI powered Windows 11, finally overtaking Windows 10 in market share just this month.

We believe that more users will switch to the latest software and operating systems as Windows 10 is coming to an end and because of the latest AI enhanced Copilot features. MSFT continues to innovate and provide more useful features of Copilot, and we view this as a tailwind for the stock as more users upgrade their machines to the latest and greatest operating system from MSFT. A recent study by Jefferies shows that nearly 82% of enterprises are now using Copilot in some fashion, which was up from just 77% in the past fall. This growth will help MSFT continue to be a leader in AI and computing, and we are happy to see them in this fund.

Apple (AAPL): AAPL has been a laggard in the AI race compared to some of its big tech peers. They have lost some talent to other competitors in this AI arms race, and it seems that for now, the firm does not have a strong, decisive plan on how to execute in this new era of computing and software. It certainly is disappointing that the company that gave us Siri, one of the first and most widespread “AI assistants” all the way back in 2011 on the iPhone 4s seems to have not pressed its lead and developed its capabilities further. This has been reflected in their YTD share performance, as they have underperformed SPY and QQQ, as shown in the chart below.

However, the firm does have an upcoming catalyst in their latest phone release this fall. With their preview of iOS 26, a massive change in their entire UX, we are hoping that the company gets a boost from this new product launch and that the firm gets back to its winning days of being a leader in the big tech space. AAPL definitely has underperformed this year, but they certainly do have both the legacy and the cash to turn things around in the future.

Valuation

We look at the valuation metrics of VGT’s top holdings to get a directional idea of the fund’s valuation. The goal here isn’t to calculate an exact valuation but instead to understand if the fund looks generally fairly valued, overvalued, or undervalued. We look at the top 10 names which make up 58.84% of the fund. All 10 names are in the same sector of Information Technology so we use that as the sector comparison for all.

We see that compared to the sector median P/E of 28.08, all but 1 of the top 10 names look overvalued, some by a lot. The weighted average P/E of the top 10 names is calculated by weighing each firm's P/E by its size relative to the other 9 names in VGT. The weighted average P/E ratio for all names is 67.72 which is 141.16% higher than the industry median.

We also look at the P/S ratio as it is often a more appropriate valuation metric especially for faster growing companies:

The last thing we do to confirm valuation is remove potential outliers. In both cases, PLTR was a big outlier. When recalculating the weighted average ratios without PLTR included we see:

Even after removing the huge outlier, the rest of the top 9 names show to be significantly overvalued compared to the rest of the Information Technology market.

Risks to our Thesis

AI Growth Continues to be exponential

All of the top holdings of VGT are benefitted by growth in AI in one way or the other. Some like AMD and NVDA provide the chips to run AI models while others like MSFT and PLTR use AI in their products. If AI growth does not slow and continues to be exponential, the lofty valuations of the top holdings may be more justified.

Government funding of AI growth

The Trump administration has made a point of showcasing US leadership and capabilities in the AI space, and if there is more global governmental engagement in AI, and more funding, the entire sector will benefit tremendously.

Conclusion

Overall, we love the companies included in VGT. They are top players in their fields and represent some of the most innovative and iconic companies in the world. We believe that in the long run, these companies, and therefore, VGT, should be a great investment.

However, even the best companies can be bad investments depending on their price. We see that across both P/E and P/S and even after removing outliers that the top names show lofty valuations compared to the rest of the market. The high valuations prevent us from recommending VGT as a buy at the time being, but we will be keeping an eye on the fund for if/when prices come down. We believe that at the current time, these frothy valuations make VGT an unwise investment choice. Wait for a solid dip, and then rip into big tech.

data.title: JetBlue Airways: JetForward And Weak Dollar Will Support Turnaround
data.description:
JetBlue (NASDAQ:JBLU) first caught my attention in late 2023 when airlines were trading at rock-bottom prices following the rebound after COVID. At the time, I thought airlines were a contrarian bet, mainly because Wall St was expecting solid profits and Price to Earnings Ratio (PE) were historically low. Fast forward two years, and Delta Air Lines (DAL), United Airlines (UAL) and Alaska Airlines (ALK) did make a strong comeback, while American Airlines (AAL), Southwest (LUV) and JetBlue failed to do so.

It seems that even more now, JetBlue is a significant contrarian bet, with everyone labeling the company a sell and Factors Grades pointing to a worsening of Momentum and Revisions. The only two bright spots seem to be Valuation and Growth. In this article I'll provide the contrarian argument, stating why I believe JetBlue could turnaround its business, mainly through achieving its targeted improvements from JetForward and benefiting from a possible tailwind related to shift from international to domestic leisure travel.

Investors should be aware that investing in an airline that is currently not in its best financial shape is always tricky and prone to volatility. Still, JetBlue does have a plan that investors can follow during the earnings call and assess its probability of success. Given the possibility of returning to profitability with a Net Income of more than $500M and current Market Cap of $1.6B, I believe JetBlue is a Buy for investors who like to make contrarian bets and have the stomach for the future volatility that this ride entails.

Its Origins And Why It Was Successful in the 2000s and early 2010s

JetBlue was founded in 1999, making its first flight in 2000 and doing an IPO in 2002. The main proposal of the company was to be a Low-Cost Carrier (LCC), but not an Ultra-Low-Cost Carrier (ULCC), by offering assigned seating, free snacks, live TV, comfy seats and other perks, all at a reasonable price. Its primary hub at JFK Airport, New York City supported the company's focus in the Northeast of the United States to service leisure destinations.

JetBlue's timing to start operations was definitely troubled since the US experienced a short recession following the dot-com bubble and the entire airline sector was devastated following the September 11 terrorist attacks. However, like many upstarting airlines, JetBlue had a flexible workforce that was not unionized and a small but growing fleet, which helped them navigate through a couple of years of lower demand. It is a sign of how good JetBlue strategy was that Delta and United launched low-cost subsidiaries to compete with JetBlue, but both experiences ended during the 2007-2008 financial crisis.

So right after the 2008 financial crisis, while American Airlines (AAL) was busy going through a bankruptcy and then merging with US Airways, United Airlines was merging with Continental, and Delta Airlines was integrating Northwest Airlines, JetBlue was using its newer fleet and low-cost workforce to expand and gain share. It was around that time that an MIT study coined the phrase "JetBlue Effect" to reference the fact that airfares used to drop significantly once the airline started operating in a given city. And let's be honest, JetBlue model was growing at exactly the right time: after the 2008 financial crisis, customers were not feeling good about the economy and the dollar was weak against other currencies. This helped the domestic leisure market, mainly through LCC's, to boom.

JetBlue then reached its peak Net Income and Profitability in 2016 following years of market share gain and disrupting legacy carriers. The problem was that this model started to show signs of weakness. 2015 marked the year when US residents flying abroad surpassed the 2007 peak. It was also around that time that legacy carriers started to offer more premium seats, like Delta's Premium Select. After the peak, Net Income and Profitability started to slip until COVID finally took it to negative territory. Since then, the company has been unable to turn a profit. The bar graph below smooths earnings in 2017, by adjusting the income tax benefit of $226M, and 2024, by removing $591M in Special Items.

Although the shift towards international travel and premiumization helped, the domestic leisure market didn't disappear during this period. JetBlue could have probably continued to turn a profit with its business model, if it weren't for significant high operating expenses after 2015. Jet fuel and Salaries are the main reasons the company is now unprofitable and I'll deep dive in those two in the next section.

JetBlue's Downturn

Investors who may have seen articles I wrote about other airlines might recognize the financial analysis of this section. But while for other airlines I used 2019 as comparison, for JetBlue I'll use 2016 as it was its most profitable year on record. Note that even though JetBlue is making ~$2.5B more in Revenue, its Net Income is $1.6B less in 2024 than in 2016.

There are basically three reasons that higher Revenue hasn't translated in higher Net Income: Salaries & Wages, Aircraft Fuel and Special Items. The Net Income Walk below shows it very well. Starting from the latter, Special Items is likely to disappear in 2025 as it was 90% related to the failed Spirit merger. That isn't much solace because once this company becomes profitable again, Income Tax will recover, so whatever improvement happens with Special Items we can bundle with a worsening on Income Tax, leaving the other two reasons as the main ones.

One of my favorite graphs when analyzing airlines is the Jet Fuel Price trend from IATA & S&P Global Commodity Insights. It seems crazy to remember, but Jet Fuel Price was amazingly low during 2016, starting at $40 and ending at $60, for an average of $50 during the year. This explain why Aircraft Fuel has eaten so much of JetBlue's profitability, since the average during 2024 was around $100. The good news is that, barring the spike in late June, the trend is going towards below $80, likely making the average for the year closer to $80. This is still significantly higher than the 2016 average, but shows that Aircraft Fuel could definitely still improve and support a couple of percentage points improvement in JetBlue's Net Income.

Finally, we arrive at Salaries & Wages, which is definitely the main villain. Between 2012 and 2024, JetBlue's Revenue increased by 86%, but Salaries & Wages increased by a staggering 212%. It passed from 21.0% of JetBlue's Revenue to more than 35% - for comparison, American, Delta and United are all close to ~30%. I'm sorry if there is any pilot here, but the problem likely started when the pilots decided to unionize, which took effect in 2015. They received a 3.5% raise in 2016, an 18.5% raise in 2018 and a 21.5% raise in 2023. This translates close to a 50% compounded increase.

JetBlue was used to underprice competitors since it had a significantly lower personnel cost due to its relatively new operation and non-unionized workforce. Both advantages have disappeared and in the meantime the company was unable to increase its Revenue like Delta and United did through premium seats and additional perks. Salaries won't come down, so JetBlue presented a plan called JetForward to maximize its Revenue and recover profitability.

JetForward Plan To Boost Profitability

At least one thing I can give JetBlue right away: they built a plan to maximize Revenue rather than simply cutting costs, which is called JetForward. If it was just costs, it wouldn't work as I explained before that the majority of their expenses was committed or hard to change. Focusing on maximizing Revenue is the right move, even though JetBlue is now playing catch-up with the other airlines in terms of improving customer experience.

The slide below details the actions for four strategic drivers that JetBlue aims to achieve in order to generate $800M to $900M more in EBIT until 2027. Half of that ($400M) is focused in Premiumization, with $175M dedicated to reorganizing its routes and the rest more or less related to operational efficiency. Next I'll detail how each one is going and how feasible they are to be achieved in 2027.

Reliable & Caring Service

Target: $100M

I guess readers wouldn't be too far off if they thought this bucket was a mix of too many initiatives without a coherent KPI to measure. It might be telling that the company hasn't dedicated a single slide in three JetForward presentations to detail actions under Reliable & Caring Service. In the summary, they said the issue was that Air Traffic Control (ATC) was understaffed which caused delays in congested airports, but their solution was a general "Improving reliability by investing in on-time performance and delivering consistent customer experience".

But the company is giving us something to measure them for and uncover what they meant with this initiative. They claimed the company gained three positions in the WSJ Ranking of Airlines which is great until you notice that JetBlue was ranked ninth out of nine airlines for three years in a row. The three spots the company gained in 2024 made them "better" than Spirit (OTC:SAVEQ), Frontier (ULCC) and American Airlines. Let's be honest: a sixth place and in front of these competitors is the least that JetBlue needs to achieve since it's very proud of having a good customer service.

The WSJ Ranking measures things like On-Time Arrivals (JetBlue went from 9th to 8th), Canceled Flights (9th to 3rd), Extreme Delays (9th to 8th), Tarmac Delays (9th to 7th), Mishandled Baggage (6th to 2nd), Involuntary Bumping (4th to 5th) and Complaints (6th to 7th). The company likely considers costs related to these cancelled flights, lost baggage and accommodation to passengers under this initiative, but I find it hard to measure and to know that the company really delivered on those $100M. So for this first initiative I'm not giving too much credit and I'd honestly assume $0M incremental in my model, unless they become Top 3 in WSJ's Ranking next year.

Best East Cost Leisure Network

Target: $175M

This one is definitely more tangible, mainly because JetBlue already announced (and executed) the planned exits and entries highlighted below. Following its strategic driver, it exited several routes from Los Angeles Airport (LAX), Fort Lauderdale Airport (FLL), some between the Northeast and Midcontinent, and optimized La Guardia Airport (LGA) in New York City and Newark International Airport (EWR) in New Jersey.

Using data from the Bureau of Transportation Statistics, JetBlue reported a loss of almost $1B in domestic routes, only partially offset by close to $100M Net Income in each of Latin America and Atlantic markets. So it's no wonder that the majority of adjustments in its network is in continental United States (even when we exclude the $500M in Special Items from Spirit). Latin America was also in a better spot pre-covid, so exiting some peripheral markets like Bogotá, Quito, Guayaquil and Lima was probably for the best.

And with the extra capacity from exited routes, the company is either doubling down in existing routes from Northeast to leisure destinations or adding new ones with the same strategic focus. Also, with the new fleet and the Mint cabin they are expanding this offer even further with its Atlantic and transcontinental routes. Now we need to see this executed in 2025, but I'd give management full credit and consider the $175M in my model.

Products & Perks Customers Value

Target: $400M

This is the core of JetBlue's JetForward transformation. Sometimes it's hard for a company to abandon what made it great in the first place, but several of the initiatives announced under this bucket are just JetBlue catching up with what customers have wanted for the past ten years. For some die-hard fans of JetBlue, this looks like heresy in the level of Southwest ditching open seats and free bags. Still, it's significantly important and the key initiative to make JetBlue profitable again.

The company has already made several announcements and implemented the majority of them, like: a new partnership with United Airlines (UAL), linking both loyalty programs; added a free carry-on bag to its Blue Basic, differentiating its basic economy fee; introduce future lounges in John F Kennedy Airport (JFK) and Boston International Airport (BOS), to attract more premium passengers; launched premier credit card with Barclays (BCS), in a bid to attract more lavish customers; and, finally, revamped its cabin by introducing Domestic First, a new Even More product and Preferred Seating.

This is probably the biggest update, since JetBlue was proud for a longtime in not offering a First Class in domestic flights. However, the company likely noticed how profitable those premium seats are within its Atlantic routes, so it decided that starting 2026 all planes who currently don't have their Mint lie-flat seat will have the new Domestic First. Given the significant announcements (and, hopefully, more to come) and my belief that they are exactly in the right direction, I'm comfortable in using $300M of the $400M to model JetBlue's earnings in our valuation section. More will likely depend on JetBlue's own performance through 2025 and if there are any new announcements.

A Secure Financial Future

Target: $175M

Lastly, JetBlue highlighted actions to improve its Balance Sheet, mainly focusing on Aircraft Deferral (i.e. postponing Capital Expenditures) and raising fresh debt to improve liquidity. Slide below shows the main actions related to Aircraft, which were focused on deferring the deliveries of 44 A321neo to after 2029 in order to postpone $3B of Capex, accelerating deliveries of A220 to retire remaining E190 (last flight on September 4) and extending the useful life of some A320. JetBlue also highlighted the impact of grounded aircraft due to GTF engine issue in 2024 and 2025.

The issue here is that as slide 12 of this presentation shows, the company also raised additional $3B in Debt to improve its liquidity position. This has increased its Interest Expense in $100M per quarter when we compare Q2'24 vs Q1'25, which means that even if JetBlue achieves the proposed $175M from this initiative, it will then be partially offset by this increase of $100M. Since we are first reviewing the EBIT portion, I'll take the $175M at face value, but will discount the $100M later in the valuation section.

Travel Demand and Weak Dollar

Finally, even if JetBlue does everything right with JetForward, it's always good to have a good tailwind or macroeconomic development on its side. I believe JetBlue could benefit from a shift in demand from international to domestic leisure stemming from a weaker dollar. It has been widely reported that the European travel slump in the first half of 2025 was due to tough border policy and the political climate within the United Sates. I believe this is completely wrong.

First, as you can see from the USD:EUR exchange rate below, the Dollar appreciated significantly in the second half of 2024, likely discouraging travelers to book their flights to the US amid fears of a more expensive travel. Second, this article is great in highlighting that the so-called slump was actually due to Easter being earlier in 2024 and thus boosting last year's arrivals. The trend seems to have normalized after Easter, with European demand recovering although still slightly below last year.

Still, the drop in USD:EUR during the first half of 2025 makes me wonder if some US travelers will skip their next European vacation in favor of a domestic one. Americans may decide that Caribbean or Florida destinations offer more value for the same dollar. This would definitely be a boost to JetBlue and something I'll keep an eye on for future developments.

Valuation

A JetBlue Valuation is definitely a forward looking exercise as currently the company has negative net income and an uncertain path to turn it around. The walk below shows that JetBlue's adjusted Net Income in FY'24 was $(204)M after excluding the Special Items. Starting from this base:

  • Added $650M instead of the $800M to $900M the JetForward plan assumed, mainly because I'm discounting some initiatives in part to keep a conservative scenario.
  • Added back the (2.5)p. impact in Operating Margin FY'24 that JetBlue guided as coming from the GTF Engine Issue, which should be largely resolved by 2026/2027.
  • Improved Jet Fuel as % of Revenue by 2p. to consider the improvement from an average of $100 in 2024 to $80 in 2025, but a 20% drop could be even a higher benefit than the 2p.
  • Subtracted both incremental interest expense from the $3B Debt issue and the Income Tax the company is likely to pay once it turns a profit.

With all those assumptions in place our projected future Net Income for JetBlue is around $600M. The timeline for this is likely 2027 as it was the guidance the company projected within its JetForward plan. Which brings us to the issue that we are two and a half years away from the end of 2027, and if the company takes that long to deliver the stock price will likely not move up by much in the near future. Still, with $600M of projected Net Income in FY'27 without considering any upside from higher demand for domestic leisure, JetBlue trades at less than 3x Price to Earnings (PE) Ratio of FY'27 earnings. With Wall St estimating $(0.32) of EPS for FY'27, readers need to decide if JetBlue is too optimistic with JetForward, if I'm also too optimistic, or if the street is too pessimistic.

One final note needs to be in Free Cash Flow where the company may struggle a little bit down the road to generate meaningful cash for dividends or buybacks. After its $3B Capital Expenditures deferral, JetBlue has, based on its 10-K, around $1.2B in Capital Expenditures for FY'25, $0.9B in FY'26 and $0.6M between FY'27 and FY'29. Since with $600M of Net Income their Cash Flow from Operations should be around $1.2B, there won't be anything left in FY'25 and then gradually something between $0.3B and $0.6B will be available for debt repayment. This is enough to not put the company under financial stress, but not sufficient to start rewarding shareholders with dividends and buybacks.

Risks

There are several and significant risks with my projections. The main one is that all is based on JetBlue's JetForward plan and its ability to execute and deliver on EBIT improvement. Investors need to follow this with laser focus in each quarterly earnings. Signs of weakness or of delayed results from JetForward are likely to signal that this turnaround may not be achievable. I've tried to decrease some of the expectations related to JetForward to see if JetBlue would still be a good value investment, but there is always the chance that I was too optimistic.

Other risks that are always present but important to remember are related to the economy and travel demand. Airlines are extremely sensible to the economic cycle, working almost like a leading indicator when things turn bad (but usually as a lagging indicator when things are improving). In any case, airlines are volatile, so any softness in demand or economic hiccups could put additional pressure on JetBlue's valuation.

Finally, although I didn't cover extensively the Free Cash Flow, it comes as a given that if the company is unable to produce meaningful additional Net Income or Cash from Operations from JetForward it will likely be unable to service both its Capital Expenditures requirements and its Debt. This, as we all know, is poison for airlines and could put JetBlue into a downward spiral.

Q2 Earnings Preview

My focus in Q2'25 Earnings will be on the continued execution of JetForward and if there are any additional announcements that can enhance the possibility of JetForward being fully delivered. I'd also expect that JetBlue has guidance or results in line with what Delta and United reported recently, showing that the bump in travel demand during early Q2 was simply just a bump.

Other important developments are related to confidence in returning aircraft impacted by GTF engine issue to service and improving conditions related to Jet Fuel, hopefully pushing Net Income upwards. In all, Q2'25 is not expected to be the quarter to solve all of JetBlue's problems. Investors should expect continued improvement, but not out of the park earnings.

Takeaway

My expectation was to do a deep dive on JetBlue's JetForward and the company's capacity to turn things around. I do believe there is a path, but it's definitely not a certainty. Still, investors who have the stomach for the ride and agree that $4~$5 is an interesting entry point could be richly rewarded down the road. Great execution could really deliver strong earnings and allow the stock to rebound, potentially 100% to 200% when considering 6x PE or 10x PE. Even so, investors can't forget the risks I have highlighted and need to consider them in their own risk-reward analysis of JetBlue.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Contrarian Idea investment competition, which runs through July 28. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: Winnebago: Winning Is Still Possible With Its Improved Product Mix And Pricing Strategies
data.description:
The RV industry is still navigating a risky environment amid the stubborn inflation, tariff woes, and oil price volatility. Two months after my initial coverage, Winnebago Industries, Inc. (NYSE:WGO) still feels the RV market’s softness. Yet, there have been some improvements in its operational stability through its product mix and pricing strategies. That is why I believe that the stock is severely undervalued after a huge price reduction. Technicals are still weak, but there are some buying opportunities today.

WGO: Challenged Yet Resilient

During Q3 2025, Winnebago Industries, Inc. experienced mixed results amid the softness of the RV market. Yet, its strategic product diversification and strategies allowed it to stabilize its performance. Its operating revenue amounted to $775.1M, down by -1.4% YoY from $786.0M. This was mainly due to its weaker performance in the motorhome segment. Motorhome volume plunged by -14.8% YoY. Its motorhome shipments were even weaker than the market in April and May.

Despite this, there have been some improvements in revenue stability. In the past eight quarters, except for Q4 2024, WGO suffered from a massive YoY revenue drop. But in Q3 2025, the rate of decrease became much more manageable.

This can be attributed to its prudent product mix and pricing structure. We can see how it effectively managed its Towable RVs. WGO bolstered its demand by reducing prices by an average of -6.0%. As a result, total towable shipments rose by 2.5% or 232 units. But in my view, this was also driven by its decision to increase its focus on what it does best- that’s travel trailers. These are generally cheaper than fifth wheels, so these are more affordable for those who wish to travel with minimal spending. I’ll discuss it more later. We can see that it comprised 69.2% of the product mix compared to 66.1% in the previous year. In addition, its travel trailers outperformed the market. Its volume rose by 7.3% versus the -2.4% drop in the market. Note that I added the travel trailer shipments in the same period at 26,482, 24,737, and 18,376 from the RVIA reports. So, its travel trailer market share increased from 8.6% in 2024 to 9.4% in the current FY.

Its marine segment also supported its revenue growth. This was the strongest segment of WGO considering the 4.8% increase in shipments despite raising boat prices by 3.0% YoY. As a result, marine segment revenue rose from $87.9M to $100.7M or by 14.6% YoY. While this was not enough to offset the weakness in motorhomes, this still showed new sprinkles of hope for the company.

Mitigants/Drivers

In a recent survey, it was found that the American revenge travel trend is already over, so we may expect lower travel demand this year. This was supported by another study wherein 54% of Americans will slash their travel spending. This was mainly due to the stubborn inflation that affects purchasing power and discretionary spending.

In response, 43% of Americans intend to drive to their destination and stay local. This can open more opportunities for RVs to regain their market appeal. In another study, 44M Americans plan to travel by RV. It is also worth noting that 83% of Millennials and 81% of Gen Zers are enthusiastic about RV travel this summer. In fact, younger generations are leading online RV searches.

With that in mind, WGO may capitalize on the resilient demand for travel trailers. One important aspect is its cheapness relative to motorhomes. From an average price of $41,700 per unit, WGO lowered it to $39,156. So, it’s not surprising that volume rose. As a result, the increased volume offset the decrease in prices. The actual revenue of Travel Trailers reached $257.2M, up by 0.7% from $255.3 YoY. This shows that WGO still affords to lower prices to bolster demand for its strong products.

I also expect its marine segment to remain a primary growth driver. Why? Somehow, it is insulated from the impact of inflation due to the fact that it’s a luxury good with less available alternatives unlike RVs. Its target market is often individuals from high-income households or high-net-worth individuals. They have a more resilient purchasing power despite inflationary headwinds.

Another core strength of the company is its decent liquidity through its effective cash and debt management. Currently, its Net Debt/EBITDA is 4.76x, which is quite higher than the ideal maximum of 4.5x. This means that WGO needs about five years to repay all debt at once. Despite this, we must note that it has a long debt maturity profile. In fact, the majority of its borrowings are convertible notes maturing in 2030. Hence, it still has enough capital buffer and time to repay them on time.

NOPAT Projection

To assess WGO further, I estimated its performance starting from its revenues. I could have used its 4.8% CAGR. However, I think it’s too early to be optimistic. Also, it will be inconsistent with its softer performance for three quarters of FY 2025. Instead, I based my estimation on its quarterly performance and derived $2.724B. This is in line with the company guidance of $2.7-2.8B. For its operating costs and expenses, I set them as a percentage of revenue at 85% and 10%, respectively. These are also based on its historical performance. To be conservative, I increased them by 2.0% to add the impact of inflation. Lastly, I got depreciation and capex using the straight-line method. So, NOPAT will only be $51M. Despite this, WGO will stay profitable, proving its resilience in the long run.

For 2026-2029, I decided to use the 4.8% CAGR to derive revenues. I believe the market will be more stable by that time. From COGS to depreciation, I used the same methods. In the end, NOPAT will rebound, although the value will stay lower than in 2021 to 2022. In my view, this is reasonable since RVs boomed in these years.

Valuation

After three months, the value of WGO continues to slip as its performance remains relatively soft. Despite this, I believe it opens more opportunities to buy shares at a discount. Using P/B, the price trades at only 0.72x its price, which is very cheap in my opinion. This is lower than the standard P/B of 1.0x. This is also lower than the five-year average of 1.69x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP will be $73.52. However, I think it’s too high. If we use the standard P/B of 1.00x, the TP will become more reasonable at $43.43. The same goes for P/S, which shows that the stock only trades at 0.72x its sales per share. If we use the average P/S, we will derive $54.93 as its TP.

To have a more precise estimation, I also used the DCF Model. This time, I believe the TP is more reasonable at $38.06 since I based it on my NOPAT projection. This is more conservative than my previous estimation using CAGR. I still set its perpetual growth at 2.00%, while risk premium is 5.00%. WACC is still derived using the same method. Lastly, I added a 5% risk consideration to be more conservative, so I got $36.16. This is still higher than the current price, suggesting a reasonable upside potential.

Technicals

WGO’s momentum remains weak today as rebound attempts remain short-lived. Yet, we can see recent improvements as the price stabilizes after the correction. We can see that the 50-day SMA has already flattened after its steep downtrend. This shows that market sentiments have already neutralized.

MACD and RSI also suggest the same trend but show new buying opportunities. Although MACD remains within negative territory, it has kept increasing in the past three weeks. It is also getting closer to positive territory after crossing above the signal line. Meanwhile, the histogram trends upward and remains in positive territory, showing that bulls are entering after the recent dip. Lastly, RSI shows there is still enough room for buying.

Conclusion

Winnebago Industries, Inc. remains challenged amid a softer market environment. But its prudent product mix and pricing strategies offer it new opportunities for recovery. This justifies its upside potential amid its underpricing. Lastly, technicals are weak, but buying opportunities are emerging. I’m reiterating the buy rating on this stock.

data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: NIQ Flops And Accelerant Shines In 7 IPO Week
data.description:
Seven IPOs debuted this week, while one small IPO and five SPACs submitted initial filings.

Consumer intelligence provider NIQ Global Intelligence (NIQ) priced its IPO below the midpoint to raise $1.1 billion at a $6.2 billion market cap. The company is a leading global consumer intelligence company, and its global reach spans over 90 countries, covering approximately 85% of the world's population, more than half the world's GDP, and more than $7.2 trillion in global consumer spend as of December 31, 2024. It has grown its revenue in recent years, aided by strong retention rates, although it remains loss-making, with a negative free cash flow. NIQ Global Intelligence fell 6%.

Specialty insurance marketplace Accelerant (ARX) priced its upsized IPO above the range to raise $724 million at a $4.7 billion market cap. Accelerant operates a data-driven risk exchange that connects selected specialty insurance underwriters (sellers) with risk capital partners (buyers). On the "supply side" of its Risk Exchange, the company delivers a full service offering to members, while on the "demand side," the company offers risk capital partners a portfolio of specialty insurance premium that may otherwise be difficult to access elsewhere. As of March 31, 2025, Accelerant had 232 members and 96 risk capital partners on its platform. It finished the week up 42%.

Leading educational publisher McGraw Hill (MH) priced its IPO below the range to raise $415 million at a $3.3 billion market cap. McGraw Hill is a leading global provider of information solutions for education across K-12 to higher education, and through professional learning. On an annual basis, the company serves approximately 60 million learners and educators. McGraw Hill is a top two provider in the K-12 market in the US, serving approximately 99% of public K-12 districts. It is also a top two provider of digital and print learning solutions in the US higher education market, serving approximately 82% of US higher education institutions, although it is highly leveraged at 3.8x net debt/EBITDA. It finished the week down 1%.

Spine surgery-focused medtech Carlsmed (CARL) priced at the midpoint to raise $101 million at a $430 million market cap. Its aprevo Technology Platform consists of AI-enabled software solutions, interbody implants custom designed for each patient's unique pathology and vertebral bone topography, and single-use surgical instruments. The company currently markets the aprevo Technology Platform for lumbar spine fusion surgery, and it is further developing the Platform for use in cervical spine fusion surgeries, which it expects to commercialize in 2026. Carlsmed finished the week down 3%.

Hong Kong-based events management services provider Dreamland (TDIC) priced its US IPO at the bottom of the range to raise $8 million at a $124 million market cap. Dreamland provides event management services in Hong Kong for a range of public and private events, from trade shows, conferences, and concerts, to internal corporate events. Its activities include assisting with organizing, planning, promoting, selling merchandise, and managing events. Dreamland finished the week down 6%.

Hong Kong-based financial printing firm Cre8 Enterprise (CRE) priced its downsized US IPO at the bottom of the range to raise $6 million at a $96 million market cap. The company provides financial printing services for listed companies, IPO applicants and private companies in the finance and capital market in Hong Kong. Its services include concept creation and artwork design, typesetting, proofreading, printing, and stock exchange submission services. Cre8 Enterprise finished the week up 9%.

Real estate platform Linkhome Holdings (LHAI) priced its upsized IPO at the proposed price to raise $6 million at a $64 million market cap. Through its subsidiaries, Linkhome operates a real estate platform with the goal of providing customers with end-to-end solutions and services, initially comprising real estate brokerage services, Cash Offer, and other services like property rental management and home renovation. data.title: The Interesting Case Of The Hong Kong Bull Run
data.description:
There has been a decided thaw in the economic cold war between the US and China. Just last week, megatech chipmaker Nvidia (NVDA), the first company to reach a $4 trillion market capitalization, got a boost to its already copious fortunes when the government lifted restrictions on its ability to sell its H20 chips to China. Those restrictions had previously forced the company into a $4.5 billion writedown from first quarter results.

More broadly, the lifting of export restrictions for Nvidia reflects a growing sense among investors that the bellicose rhetoric of several months ago is turning into a softer cadence of looking for dealmaking opportunities.

That, in turn, is bringing back into play the question of China as an investable opportunity. Front and center in this debate is one of the year’s best-performing stock markets to date: Hong Kong.

The Window on China

Hong Kong’s role as a window on China – a proxy for those unwilling or unable to venture into the mainland itself – goes way, way back. Back to the days when multinational entrepots like Jardine Matheson and Swire’s – the historical inspirations for James Clavell’s saga “Tai Pan” – mediated between the pecuniary interests of foreign investors and the ever-changing faces of Chinese power from emperors to nationalists, red book-waving communists and Deng Xiaoping-era reformists.

That storied history has taken a hit in recent years as the heavy political hand of Beijing weighed on the freewheeling ways of the Cantonese-speaking island. But there are definite signs of a resurgence, not least of all in the form of the Hang Seng stock index thus far in 2025.

Connecting to the Mainland

What’s behind the strong performance of Hong Kong this year, and why is it outpacing mainland Chinese equity indexes, represented in the above chart by the Shenzhen A Share index (green line)? To answer this, it is worth noting that Hong Kong serves as an opportunity for investors in mainland China – both institutions and individuals – to invest in Chinese companies while avoiding the tightly-controlled domestic financial system.

They can do this via a mechanism called Stock Connect. Many of the most sought-after Chinese equity names – the likes of tech giants Alibaba (BABA) and Baidu (BIDU) – are available in Hong Kong via Stock Connect but are not quoted on mainland exchanges.

This year, over $104 billion has been invested into Hong Kong from mainland sources, more than the amount invested over the entire year last year. Perhaps unsurprisingly, this has been happening as new listings by Chinese companies in Hong Kong are also at a record: 208 companies in the first six months of 2025, making Hong Kong the largest IPO market in the world so far this year, with $13.9 billion in deal flow compared to $9.2 billion on Nasdaq, $7.8 billion on the NYSE and less than half a billion in London.

Absent at the Feast

What is missing from this picture – or at least, what is not dominating the picture so far – is meaningful investment flows from origins other than China. This goes back to that question we introduced earlier: is China investable? There have been plenty of reasons for international investors to answer that question in the negative. The Chinese economy has been in the doldrums for years now, with the chronic sickness of its all-important property market and lackluster consumer spending that keeps the economy teetering on the brink of deflation.

But there are longer-term strategic reasons to question this hitherto conventional wisdom. China has a long-term strategy predicated on achieving superiority in the so-called industries of tomorrow: artificial intelligence, clean energy and biotechnology, among others.

China’s carmaking industry is now world class, particularly in the electric vehicle segment, where it is the number one global producer. The fortunes of leading carmaker BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) are rising as those of Tesla (TSLA) are falling. The export-driven economy has managed to withstand the pain inflicted by the trade war – which, as noted above, appears to be softening.

Exposure to Hong Kong equities is not particularly difficult for international investors. Exchange-traded funds like the iShares MSCI Hong Kong ETF (EWH) – or the MSCI Pacific ex-Japan (EPP) variant if one wants some added diversification into other regional markets (Singapore, Australia and New Zealand alongside Hong Kong) – are liquid and cost-efficient.

The risks are still there, of course. But as a long-term strategy, the China case study is not without merit. Hong Kong, in its time-honored role as a window on China – is a potentially attractive way to gain a foothold.

data.title: Private Equity Exits Tilted Toward Trade Sales In H1 2025
data.description:
While private equity exits via trade sales were down slightly in the first half of 2025, corporate acquirers continued to strike deals with private equity fund managers scrambling to monetize aging portfolio company investments.

Exits to corporate strategics totaled 1,191 in the first half, down 3% from 1,231 trade sales during the same period in 2024, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data. By comparison, first-half IPOs of private equity portfolio companies, another key exit route, fell 31% from the year-ago period.

Private equity-backed exits by type, 2020–2025

By value ($B)

By volume

Data compiled July 24, 2025.

Analysis includes largest global deals completed between Jan. 1, 2020, and June 30, 2025, where the seller is or includes a private equity or venture capital firm. Excludes terminated deals.

* Year to date through June 30, 2025.

Source: S&P Global Market Intelligence.

© 2025 S&P Global.

After several years of relatively slow exit activity, private equity fund managers face growing pressure to cash in aging investments and return capital to their investors. Corporate acquirers turned that dynamic to their advantage, said Scott Voss, a managing director at HarbourVest Partners LLC.

"The strategics are active, and they're taking advantage of the lack of competition from other liquidity sources," Voss said.

Strategic advantage

Corporates tend to move more slowly than private equity firms in M&A transactions. Despite this, corporates are benefiting from a complex and rapidly shifting macroeconomic environment that has restricted the competition, said Jason Strife, head of junior capital and private equity solutions for Churchill Asset Management LLC, a lender to private equity-owned businesses.

"Strategics probably do better in a market like we have now, simply because private equity buyers aren't moving as fast as they typically do," Strife said.

Strong public market performance also means corporations "certainly have cash to pay for acquisitions," Strife added.

Largest exits

During the first half of the year, six of the 10 private equity exits with the largest reported transaction values were sales to corporate acquirers. That includes the top deal on the list: the $18.08 billion deal that merged brokerage Haitong Securities Co. Ltd. (OTCPK:HTNGF) with Guotai Junan Securities Co. Ltd. (OTCPK:GJJSY), forming Guotai Haitong Securities Co. Ltd. Haitong Securities was backed by Shanghai Guosheng Group Co. Ltd., China CITIC Financial Asset Management Co. Ltd. (OTCPK:CHUAF) and Qilu Zhongtai Private Equity Fund Co. Ltd.

Pause button

Voss said optimism for an uptick in exit activity was high at the start of 2025, especially after 2024 finished with a greater than 9% increase in the number of global exits. Expectations that momentum would carry into 2025 were dashed when US tariff policy rattled global M&A markets and private equity dealmakers.

Exits via trade sales quieted in the second quarter as global markets digested the impact of US tariffs. The number of portfolio companies sold to corporate acquirers and the value of those deals declined in the April–June period compared with the prior three months.

IPOs were a relative bright spot in the exit market, with the second quarter's $8.98 billion in global private equity-backed IPOs representing the best quarterly mark since the first quarter of 2024.

Private equity-backed exits by type, 2020–2025

By value ($B)

By volume

Data compiled July 1, 2025.

Analysis includes largest global deals completed between Jan. 1, 2020, and June 30, 2025, where the seller is or includes a private equity or venture capital firm. Excludes terminated deals.

* Quarter to date through June 30, 2025.

Source: S&P Global Market Intelligence.

© 2025 S&P Global.

"April 2 hit and everyone pressed pause for a period of time," Voss said, referring to the date President Donald Trump imposed a new round of tariffs on key US trading partners.

The pause in deal activity only increased the pressure fund managers are feeling to make exits and increase the flow of capital back to investors, whose distributions from private equity investments have slowed in recent years.

One source of relief has been the secondary market, where private equity fund stakes are bought and sold, typically at a discount. Secondary deals surged in 2024, but the market is facing a potential mismatch between the demand for deals and the amount of capital available to meet that demand.

The value of private equity secondary transactions totaled $40.14 billion globally through April 30, according to Preqin's latest data.

Private equity-backed exit by secondary buyout

Yearly

Quarterly

Data compiled July 1, 2025.

* Year to date through April 30, 2025.

Source: Preqin Pro.

© 2025 S&P Global.

Outlook

The slow reopening of the IPO window is boosting the outlook for private equity exits in the second half of the year and beyond.

Blackstone Inc. CEO Stephen Schwarzman said on a recent earnings call that the company was preparing several portfolio company IPOs amid a brighter overall picture for exit activity.

Voss said a cut to interest rates in the US, private equity's largest market, would likely boost M&A activity and could be key to unlocking exits. "If you're a patient buyer, wait until you can just borrow 50 or 100 basis points less than what you're able to borrow at today," Voss said.

Josh Smigel, US private equity lead for PwC, said most of his firm's clients are not waiting for significant interest rate cuts.

Clients are more focused on the need for clarity about the Trump administration's economic plans, particularly around tariffs, Smigel said.

data.title: SentinelOne Is Ripe For A Takeover, But Still Not A Buy
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier this week, SentinelOne (NYSE:S) stock surged by a double-digit percentage after rumors of a $7-10 billion acquisition by cybersecurity giant Palo Alto Networks (PANW) circulated in the financial media.

Now, while Palo Alto Networks has reportedly denied the deal speculation as untrue rumors via CNBC, SentinelOne stock holding onto a decent chunk of its pop is quite interesting. Is Mr. Market sniffing out a deal?

Let's assess the feasibility of such an M&A transaction and reevaluate SentinelOne stock to determine if it's a worthwhile investment here.

Does A SentinelOne-Palo Alto Networks Combo Make Sense?

As you may know, Palo Alto Networks is a cybersecurity industry bellwether that offers a comprehensive suite of solutions, including next-gen firewalls, cloud security, and endpoint security, whereas SentinelOne is widely recognized for its strong endpoint protection capabilities.

Historically, Palo Alto Networks has relied on tuck-in acquisitions to drive inorganic growth, and so, SentinelOne at $7-10B doesn't quite fit into the equation. However, Palo Alto Networks has a solid balance sheet, robust free cash flow generation, and requisite capital market support [PANW is currently trading at a significant premium to S, with ~10x revenue but ~20x market cap] to execute such a large M&A transaction.

Now, from a product standpoint, SentinelOne's Singularity platform and Palo Alto Networks' Cortex XDR platform are direct rivals in the rapidly growing, multi-billion-dollar Endpoint Detection & Response [EDR] and Extended Detection & Response [XDR] markets. While both companies have strong endpoint protection offerings, SentinelOne is among the market leaders, alongside CrowdStrike (CRWD) and Microsoft (MSFT). Hence, despite product overlap, potential integration hurdles, and likely regulatory challenges, Palo Alto Networks' pursuit of SentinelOne makes logical sense on paper.

By bringing SentinelOne into the fold, Palo Alto Networks could potentially improve its Cortex XDR platform and AI offerings [through Purple AI], whilst simultaneously gaining the opportunity to platformize SentinelOne's non-overlapping customer base onto Palo Alto Networks' broader suite of cybersecurity solutions.

Furthermore, SentinelOne is a relative bargain among cybersecurity stocks [primarily due to its lack of profitability], trading at just ~6.5x forward P/S.

With PANW currently trading at ~14.8x forward P/S, acquiring SentinelOne (even at ~10x P/S [$10B market cap], representing a 50% premium to S' current market price) could result in significant value creation for Palo Alto Networks' shareholders in short order, especially if the potential deal is structured to be paid fully or mostly in stock.

Also, while SentinelOne is still a few years away from GAAP profitability, it is a free cash flow-generative business. If sufficient M&A synergies can be identified, Palo Alto Networks could digest SentinelOne without any significant impact on its bottom line.

Now, Palo Alto Networks has apparently dismissed the acquisition rumors, but I think such a deal makes a ton of sense if regulatory and integration hurdles can be overcome. Nikesh Arora & Co. have laid out big revenue targets for the future, aiming to double the business over the next five years. And, SentinelOne looks like an enticing prospect to drive inorganic growth, at a time when Palo Alto Networks' growth rates have moderated down to the low double-digit percentages.

In my mind, SentinelOne is ripe for a strategic takeover in the near-to-medium term, and Palo Alto Networks will not necessarily be the only suitor. However, betting on a potential M&A deal cannot be an investment strategy. Let's reevaluate SentinelOne's fair value and expected return to see if S stock deserves fresh capital allocation here.

SentinelOne Fair Value And Expected Return

Back in March 2025, I initiated coverage on SentinelOne in a comparative analysis with CrowdStrike:

With both CrowdStrike and SentinelOne growing at a similar pace, I prefer CrowdStrike from a fundamental standpoint due to its greater scale and higher cash generation (optimized margins). Unfortunately, Mr. Market does too, with CRWD trading at a rich valuation of 20x+ P/S (~3x of SentinelOne's valuation multiple).

Based on our absolute valuation assessment, SentinelOne has a smaller downside risk to fair value, and a higher long-term reward potential. That said, none of CRWD and S meets TQI's investment hurdle rate of 15%. I guess we will just have to exercise more patience to get an attractive entry point.

Key Takeaway: I rate both CrowdStrike and SentinelOne "Neutral/Hold/Avoid" at current levels.

Source: CrowdStrike Vs. SentinelOne: 2 Cybersecurity Titans, No Winning Play

Since then, SentinelOne stock has remained relatively stable, despite briefly dipping to our previous fair value estimate of approximately $16 per share during the broad market sell-off in April.

Here's my updated valuation model for SentinelOne:

Note: For today's valuation exercise, I have updated SentinelOne's revenue base to FY2026E revenue of $1B. Furthermore, I have reduced our 5-year modeling period sales growth from 25% to 20%, bringing it in line with consensus analyst estimates. All other model assumptions have been held and are relatively straightforward; however, if you have any questions, please share them in the comments section below.

From an absolute valuation standpoint, SentinelOne remains slightly overvalued. More importantly, SentinelOne's long-term risk/reward as measured by its 5-year expected CAGR return of ~9% fails to meet TQI's investment hurdle rate of 15%. Hence, SentinelOne stock is still not a "Buy" under our valuation process.

Concluding Thoughts

The rumor mill suggests SentinelOne is an acquisition target for cybersecurity behemoth Palo Alto Networks. Theoretically, such an M&A transaction (even at a 50% price premium) makes sense. However, as a standalone company, SentinelOne's long-term risk/reward is not attractive enough to warrant buying S stock right here, right now.

Key Takeaway: I rate SentinelOne "Neutral/Hold" at current levels.

Thanks for reading, and happy investing. Please share your thoughts, concerns, and/or questions in the comments section below.

data.title: UPS: Can It Keep Shipping Out Its Dividend?
data.description:
Intro

United Parcel Service (NYSE:UPS) was founded in 1907 in Seattle, Washington, initially as the American Messenger Company. The company operated under this name until 1919, when it was renamed United Parcel Service, uplifting its expanded focus on package delivery services. The name change occurred as the company grew beyond local messenger services, through consolidation of operations. Parcel delivery, particularly for retail stores, became the foundation of its modern logistics business. UPS has grown into a global leader in package delivery and supply chain management.

UPS became a public company on November 10, 1999, listing on the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) under the ticker symbol UPS.

Through key acquisitions like Mail Boxes Etc. in 2001 (rebranded as The UPS Store, adding mailbox, shipping, and clerical services) and Coyote Logistics in 2015 (expanding its full truckload delivery service), UPS has continuously expanded its logistics and retail footprint. Today, the company operates in over 200 countries, delivering millions of packages daily under its iconic brown branding.

The stock of UPS has a big fanbase among dividend investors. The company only once straight at the beginning of its public journey cut its dividend. Since then, by now a quarter century, the dividend was at the minimum kept stable every year, but in most cases it was increased. Even during the turbulences of the Great Recession, the payout was not cut.

This might be about to change now. I think a painful readjustment of the dividend might be in store. As dividend stocks usually react very negatively to dividend cuts (see yesterday's reaction of Dow Inc., dropping 17%), I rate UPS a sell until the dividend is cut.

Dividend investors could become bag holders

Since being a public company, UPS' stock did not see really spectacular moves. As a cyclical business, the stock dropped during the Great Recession, and initially likewise during the 2020 lockdown shock.

But it staged a phenomenal rise, doubling in less than two years between the low in 2020 and the subsequent all-time high in early 2022. The lockdown-period was a strong boost to the business, as ordering online and higher demand for parcel shipping services experienced a strongly elevated demand.

However, since then, the stock has dropped again, back to zero so to speak, Of course not really to zero, but to where it came from at the start of the special upswing for the business.

Long-term investors are likely not very happy to see that the stock returned only 45% over a quarter century – less than 2% CAGR. But as teased in my intro, UPS is in addition to being a strongly cyclical stock, primarily a target for dividend hunters, not long-term compounders or buy-and-hold investors.

That said, I think the dividend success story might be in jeopardy.

In 1999, UPS sliced its payout from 30 cents per share and quarter to only 17 cents. Since then, the dividend has grown to $1.64 apiece, almost 10 times the initial amount. Thus it's understandable the stock is so popular among dividend investors, and supported by no further cuts having been made since.

Above, we can see that until 2022, the dividend hikes were moderate. Then suddenly, a big bump occurred.

At the moment of writing, UPS is delivering an unheard of yield of more than 6%. Abnormally high yields usually signal stress, pointing to potential dividend cuts.

This does not have to be the case, as many companies try to defend their shareholder rewards for as long as possible, at times even by stretching the balance sheet.

During the lockdowns and on the tailwind of a strongly booming business, UPS massively hiked its dividend from $1.02 to $1.52, or by almost 50%, abandoning their previously rather cautious hiking rhythm.

The issue is now that the business has strongly lost its momentum. Let's have a look at some fundamentals to understand what I mean. Starting with long-term sales, we can see that revenues have dropped back to levels from early to mid 2021, i.e. four years back.

Unfortunately, margins have fallen to lower levels than sales. Gross margins are with one exception in late 2020 currently on the lowest level over the last five years.

The picture is even worse for operating margins. Here on a rolling twelve month basis, a new low has been made.

The reason for the margin pressure is obviously a significantly higher cost base. While sales were driven by a mix of higher volumes, and price hikes for UPS's services, wages and salaries have jumped from $38 billion to $48 billion, a 26% increase.

As a reminder from above, sales are back to where they came from.

This higher cost base is not variable, while sales are. Sales have dropped, while personnel costs remain higher. While UPS announced job cuts for the first 12,000, and then 20,000 positions, these are lagging effects. Cutting jobs through natural fluctuation (workers reaching retirement age) takes much too long for noticeable cost cutting effects. Laying people off causes one-time costs that are not helpful for the finances in the short-term, causing temporarily higher costs through severance payments.

I am saying this because if the weak business momentum remains or even intensifies, causing lower sales while the high cost base remains or only falls slowly, the pressure on UPS' finances will only increase.

As my core topic is the dividend, let's have a look at it now.

In tandem with sales, operating and free cash flow are currently in a downtrend. Fiscal 2024 closed with $6.2 billion in free cash flow. The dividend cost $5.4 billion. It was covered, no issues here.

However, if we look at the rolling twelve month chart, the red light goes on. Per last count, the first quarter of 2025, the dividend was not covered anymore by FCF. By the way, in Q3 2024 this was the case either. In the more distant past, the dividend was always covered. Now the massive hike in 2022 might be a strong stress-test.

Now the big question is, in which direction will the business go. Q1 was rather weak with sales declining 0.7%. On the other hand, operating margins improved to 8.2% (from 7.4%). Management guided for sales to reach $89 billion for the year, which would be a drop of around 2%. Profitability is expected to rise with operating margins of 10.8% targeted. The reason for this optimism are ongoing restructuring and cost cutting initiatives. Through a mix layoffs, higher automation, and the acceleration of the Amazon volume glide-down, management wants to achieve these goals.

I have doubt this will be a walk in the park. The first quarter of 2025 saw a sudden drop in US GDP growth, a first step into a recession.

While the figure for Q2 2025 is expected to be a positive 2.4% by the Atlanta FED, this is likely not due to a sudden miracle, but rather the result of pre-ordering in light of the coming tariffs. Q2 will likely not be the deciding factor, but the next couple of quarters that follow.

And this is where I am seeing the danger for the dividend of UPS. With falling economic activity and weak consumer spending, UPS will be at the forefront. This can force management to lower their sales guidance, increasing the risk for the dividend to be cut.

The balance sheet per FY 2024 had $14.4 billion net debt. Per last count (Q1 2025), net debt was almost $16 billion as a result of cash dropping from $6.3 billion to $5 billion. The balance sheet is not weak per se, but due to the fact that the dividend currently is consuming the entire FCF, the financial flexibility might be limited. Taking on more debt in this fragile environment to keep up the dividend would not be a smart move.

This could prove to be a tight race. My view is that management hiked the dividend much too aggressively during the booming period, which will likely bite them now over the next quarters.

Earnings next Week

Next Tuesday, 29 July 2025, UPS will report their second quarter results. I do not expect a dividend cut then. That would be too early. UPS could even post strong numbers as a result of the pre-ordering before tariffs hit.

Investors need to watch sales development, cash on the balance sheet, operating and free cash flow, and the cost cutting measures (though bigger effects were guided for Q3 and Q4).

The really interesting period will begin thereafter. In that sense, the guidance will be important.

Risks to my Thesis

My thesis that UPS will need to cut their dividend over the next quarters is based on my expectation that the US economy will weaken after a strong Q2. If this is not be the case, and the business momentum turns up again, the dividend of UPS will likely not be in danger.

Further, if management is successful or even overdelivers on its cost-cutting actions, the breathing room could increase, despite slightly lower sales.

Management could engage in working capital gimmicks to make cash flow look higher than it is. For example, payables have dropped over the last years. It is thinkable that management "borrows" from this position to increase their cash flow. However, this would only be a temporary measure. But it could buy them some time with the potential that the economy turns back up after a few weaker quarters.

Conclusion

I view the dividend of UPS as not safe. While I am not expecting a cut to occur with the coming earnings release next week, the risk for a cut increases thereafter with my expectation being the US economy could fall into a recession in the second half.

data.title: XTN: Avoid This Low Quality Small-Cap-Leaning Transportation ETF
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I last reviewed the SPDR S&P Transportation ETF (NYSEARCA:XTN) more than three years ago on January 27, 2022, when I argued it was not worth buying due to a high degree of speculation among its underlying holdings. I haven't given the ETF much thought since, but I see that XTN has badly lagged behind several peers and the broader Industrials sector.

As shown above, two direct competitors, the iShares Transportation Average ETF (IYT) and the First Trust Nasdaq Transportation ETF (FTXR), beat XTN by 9% and 5%, while the equal-weight and market-cap-weighted versions of the S&P 500 Industrials Index (RSPN and XLI) outperformed by 50% and 54%. And while past performance does not predict the future, my analysis indicates XTN will persistently underperform its peers due to its poor quality features. I'll highlight this deficiency along with others in detail below, and as always, I look forward to your questions afterward. I hope you enjoy the read.

XTN Overview

Strategy Discussion

XTN has a 0.35% expense ratio, $150 million in assets under management, and tracks the S&P Transportation Select Industry Index, which is comprised of stocks within the S&P Total Market Index in the following sub-industries:

  • Air Freight & Logistics
  • Airport Services
  • Cargo Ground Transportation
  • Rail Transportation
  • Highways & Rail Tracks
  • Marine Transportation
  • Marine Ports & Services
  • Passenger Airlines
  • Passenger Ground Transportation

The Index is modified equal-weighted, but the "modified" part requires some clarification because the Index doesn't follow an equal-weighting scheme in the way you might think. Instead, each component has its own maximum weight that's a function of liquidity (three-month trading volume) and size (float-adjusted market cap), subject to a 4.5% cap, as described below.

That's an easy point to miss, given how the top ten holdings below list looks like it could be equal-weighted. However, six stocks (FIP, MRTN, ULCC, UHAL, HTLD, CVLG) have weights below 1%, and five of them happen to have market caps of $1 billion or less, so their low weights aren't explained by recent poor performance alone.

Although this weighting scheme results in better diversification, it has hurt shareholders in recent years. As shown, the FMC Capped Index outperformed by 2.44% per year (10.41% vs. 7.97%) over the last three years, indicating that larger companies ended up with the most gains. The FMC Capped Index also delivered higher ten-year annualized returns (9.52% vs. 7.52%), though returns prior to April 26, 2021, were backtested.

Performance Summary vs. Peers

Since FTXR's launch on September 20, 2016, XTN easily outperformed it by 19% (106.68% vs. 87.47%) but still lagged behind IYT by the same amount and delivered roughly half the total returns of RSPN and XLI.

When looking back to XTN's inception in January 2011, we see that it managed a small annualized gain over IYT (9.93% vs. 9.44%), though that was due to exceptional performance in 2012-2014. Even so, the statistics below suggest XTN is a highly volatile product. Its 23.36% annualized standard deviation figure is far higher than IYT, RSPN, and XLI, its 34.21% drawdown was 4-7% worse than those peers, and its risk-adjusted returns (Sharpe and Sortino Ratios) were also unfavorable.

XTN Analysis

Size Segment Allocations

As I mentioned earlier, XTN isn't quite an equal-weighted fund, but it still leans much more toward the small/micro-cap segments of the U.S. stock market, which, together, represent the bottom 10% by cumulative market cap. In particular, it has 23.25% assigned to micro-cap stocks, defined as stocks in the bottom 3% by cumulative market cap. That's 22.48% more than the peer group average, whereas the four others have more exposure to large-caps.

XTN's small/micro-cap lean has undoubtedly contributed to its poor returns in recent years, as the segment in general has underperformed. One likely reason is poor quality, and as we'll see shortly, XTN is no exception. However, the segment can be attractive sometimes on other factors, particularly growth, so let's take a closer look at that next.

Fundamental Analysis

The next table highlights selected fundamental metrics for XTN's top 25 holdings, which comprise 69.73% of the portfolio. Have a quick scan, and I'll offer my observations afterward.

IYT is XTN's closest peer, as they both hold stocks from all size segments and are 100% invested in the Industrials sector, per Global Industry Classification Standards. We already know IYT has outperformed over both the short- and long-term, and importantly, there are several reasons to believe that will continue, namely:

1. XTN and IYT trade at almost the same valuation (18.80x vs. 18.75x forward earnings), but IYT offers nearly triple the one-year estimated earnings growth (15.92% vs. 5.81%), and it also has a significant advantage on three-year trailing sales and earnings growth. Of particular concern is XTN's -7.95% three-year earnings per share growth rate, and it's quite disappointing that this hasn't led to a significant P/E discount.

2. XTN has a higher five-year portfolio beta (1.47 vs. 1.32), and the ETF's five-year beta tells a similar story. I check both, as reconstitutions can sometimes produce different conclusions, but in this case, they both indicate XTN is the riskier product. Furthermore, a three-year standard deviation lookback, which evaluates total risk and not just market risk, highlights how XTN has become more volatile over the last three years. A 7.81% monthly standard deviation equates to 27.05% annualized compared to 22.24% for IYT.

3. IYT beats XTN on quality, including better weighted average net margins (15.94% vs. 4.23%), return on equity (30.35% vs. 9.87%), and return on total capital (8.88% vs. 4.53%). Seeking Alpha Factor Grades summarize this nicely, and after weighting and adjusting each component's Profitability Grades on a ten-point scale, we get the following for each ETF:

  • XTN: 6.49/10
  • IYT: 8.68/10
  • FTXR: 8.85/10
  • RSPN: 8.33/10
  • XLI: 8.81/10

4. XTN's sentiment statistics are terrible at the moment. The fundamental analysis table reveals that most of its constituents missed sales expectations last quarter, delivering a -0.77% sales surprise compared to -0.05% for IYT. Furthermore, XTN's sector-adjusted EPS Revisions Score is only 4.06/10, which corresponds to a "C-" Grade, and for additional context, that score is one of the lowest among the 200+ sector ETFs I track. To be sure, neither ETF looks great at the moment, so I think it's best to avoid the industry and stick with one of the two S&P 500 Industrials Index ETFs. They're pricier, but you'll get less volatility, more growth, and higher quality in return.

Investment Recommendation

In this analysis, I considered XTN's historical performance alongside its fundamentals, which revealed substantial deficiencies on the growth, quality, and sentiment factors. Importantly, XTN has the same forward P/E as IYT, so beyond the diversification benefits that come from its unique weighting scheme, there are no good reasons to invest. Therefore, I've assigned XTN a "sell" rating. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Microsoft And Meta Earnings Previews
data.description:
The strong growth in investing in the technology infrastructure to support generative AI (GAI) has been a focus the past year and is projected to expand this year. Alphabet increased their CapEx guidance by $10 billion to $85 billion for this year after reporting Q2 2025 on July 23, 2025. While the projected OpEx growth has decreased, the much stronger pace of the expected CapEx to support the technology infrastructure for AI has caused some concern as the significant ramp in spending is projected to grow faster than revenues. Will Meta (META) and Microsoft (MSFT) follow Alphabet (GOOGL) and increase their CapEx too?

Microsoft

According to Visible Alpha consensus, total revenues expected for Q4 have remained stable since late January, driven by a resilient view of its core business segments. However, the new Azure AI Services segment is projected to remain strong for FY 2025, with consensus estimates now expecting $11.5 billion, up from $10.8 billion. Expectations for this segment in Q4 2025 have increased by almost 15% since last quarter and 30% since late January 2025. Profitability is also expected to remain resilient for the Company.

We are closely watching what the company will say about the outlook for AI and Copilot, as Microsoft’s FY 2025 CapEx numbers have continued to increase steadily since 2019. According to consensus projections, CapEx estimates are expected to nearly double from $44.5 billion in FY 2024 to currently $69.4 billion in FY 2025 and $85.1 billion in FY 2026.

Microsoft stock has traded up 28.0% since the last earnings release and is up 37.7% since January 2024. The consensus P/E for 2025 has come down from 31x last quarter and is now 29x. Could the Q4 release and FY 2026 outlook drive more meaningful outperformance in the stock?

Meta Platforms

According to Visible Alpha consensus, total revenues for Q2 are expected to be $44.8 billion, driven by solid performance in the Family of Apps segment, especially in the U.S. and Europe. Operating profit is expected to be $17.1 billion, driven by resilience in the Family of Apps and consensus losses of -$4.9 billion for Reality Labs.

For 2025, earnings expectations for operating income from the Family of Apps have increased nearly $2.0 billion to $95.0 billion, driven by higher ad revenue per DAU in the US and internationally. The management commentary on the outlook in the earnings call will be key to assessing the potential direction of revisions. In addition, the projected losses from Reality Labs for 2025 have also decreased by nearly $1.0 billion, driven by lower anticipated losses in the second half of the year.

In Q1 2025, CEO Mark Zuckerberg highlighted that the company planned to invest in servers and data centers to support AI. CapEx consensus of $67.1 billion is above the current guidance. There are questions about whether Meta will further increase their CapEx for this year and next.

META stock has been up 30.4% since the Q1 release in April and is up 106.9% since January 2024. The consensus P/E for FY 2026 has come back up to 25x from 17x earlier in the year. Will Meta remain disciplined in FY 2025 and maintain its outperformance?

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
